title: The Basin at Argenteuil (Le Bassin d'Argenteuil)
description: One of the most celebrated Impressionist paintings in the RISD Museum collection, this work depicts the busy boating basin at Argenteuil on the Seine, a favorite subject of Monet during the early 1870s. The composition captures the shimmering reflection of sailboats on deep blue water under summer light.
provenance: Purchased from the artist for 80 francs by Comte Jean de Rasty, 1877; Bernheim-Jeune, Paris; Mrs. Murray S. Danforth, Providence, 1938. Donated by Mrs. Murray S. Danforth to RISD Museum, 1942.
credit: Gift of Mrs. Murray S. Danforth

title: A Walk in the Meadows at Argenteuil
description: Painted the year before The Basin at Argenteuil, this luminous work shows a family strolling through poppy-scattered meadows near the Seine. The loose, broken brushwork and vivid color capture the transient effects of sunlight — hallmarks of Monet's fully developed Impressionist technique.
provenance: Purchased from Monet by Durand-Ruel, December 1873; Erwin Davis, New York; Durand-Ruel, 1899; Josef Stransky, New York, 1917. Acquired by RISD Museum, 1998.

title: The Seine at Giverny
description: Painted during Monet's early years at Giverny, this serene work depicts the Seine river under overcast light. The subdued palette of greens and blues, and the reflective quality of the water anticipate the atmospheric river studies Monet would pursue for the rest of his career.
provenance: Purchased from the artist by Durand-Ruel, Paris, December 1885; Wildenstein, New York. Acquired by RISD Museum from Wildenstein & Co., September 11, 1944.

title: Before the Race
medium: gouache, chalk, graphite, and charcoal on paper
description: Degas was obsessed with the horse races at Longchamp and Chantilly, returning to the subject across four decades. This work on paper combines his favored mixed media approach — gouache for color, chalk and graphite for line — to capture jockeys maneuvering their mounts before the start.
provenance: Gift of Mrs. Murray S. Danforth to RISD Museum, 1942.
credit: Gift of Mrs. Murray S. Danforth

title: Study for the Monument to Balzac
description: This bronze is one of Rodin's working studies for the monumental Balzac commission — a project that consumed him for seven years and produced radical results. The final monument, rejected on delivery in 1898, is now recognized as a watershed in modern sculpture. This study captures the novelist's powerful physique with Rodin's characteristic rough surface modeling.
provenance: Museum purchase with funds donated by Museum Associates, Museum Members and Friends, 1966.
credit: Museum purchase with funds donated by the Museum Associates, Museum Members and Friends

title: Portrait de Victor Hugo, de Face
description: Rodin made this drypoint portrait of the aging Victor Hugo during a series of studio visits in 1886, the year before the writer's death. The portrait — a rare work on paper by the sculptor — shows Hugo full-face with the intense directness Rodin brought to all his portrait subjects.

title: Portrait of the Publisher Ambroise Vollard
description: Ambroise Vollard was the most important art dealer of the early 20th century, championing Cézanne, Renoir, Picasso, and Matisse. Picasso made several portraits of Vollard across decades. This etching from the Vollard Suite — 100 prints commissioned by Vollard himself — renders the dealer with Cubist fragmentation and remarkable psychological intensity.

title: Le Chat
medium: lift-ground aquatint and drypoint on paper
description: Made during the Nazi occupation of Paris, this image of a snarling cat carries the anxious tension of Picasso's wartime work. The lift-ground aquatint technique — in which a sugar syrup drawing is etched to produce rich tonal passages — gives the creature a raw, menacing energy.
provenance: Gift of Professor and Mrs. A. David Kossoff to RISD Museum, 1981.
credit: Gift of Professor and Mrs. A. David Kossoff

title: Haitian Woman
description: Made in the final decade of Matisse's life, this lithograph reflects his lifelong interest in the figure rendered through line alone — an approach he would soon push to its extreme in the cut-outs. The subject, identified as a Haitian woman, relates to Matisse's 1930 trip to Tahiti and his enduring engagement with non-Western figures and textiles.

title: Danseuse sur un Fauteuil
description: A dancer rests in a chair, rendered with the economy of means Matisse achieved in his prints of the 1920s. The composition — figure, chair, patterned fabric — draws on the domestic interiors Matisse obsessively revisited in his Nice period work, where models posed in elaborately decorated rooms.
provenance: Gift of the Fazzano Brothers to RISD Museum, 1984.
